body: Simon Willison flags a new hole in Claude’s web_fetch design, credited to Ayush Paul, that undercuts an otherwise thoughtful defense against data exfiltration. The threat model is the “lethal trifecta”: an AI has private context from prior interactions, can read hostile online instructions, and can leak data through URLs it accesses. Anthropic’s mitigation, as described here, is to let web_fetch visit only exact URLs supplied by the user or returned by web_search. The reported attack appears to find a way around that constraint. The excerpt cuts off before the mechanics, so the severity is hard to independently size from the supplied material—but the shape of the bug is familiar and important.
dek: A clever exfiltration attack probes the boundary between safe browsing and agentic risk.
headline: Claude’s web tool has a leakier edge than advertised
why_it_matters: As assistants gain browsing tools and memory, security depends less on model vibes and more on boring, enforceable tool boundaries. This is exactly where agent infrastructure will keep breaking.

body: House Republicans released a budget plan designed to unlock $95 billion for the Iran war and the SAVE Act, according to The New York Times. The route is already described as rocky: Republican divisions could complicate passage, and the timing would put war-funding votes in front of lawmakers months before the midterm elections. The Times also frames the war as deeply unpopular in polling, which makes the proposal not just a fiscal maneuver but a political stress test for the party.
dek: The budget plan would tee up votes before the midterms, but GOP unity is not a given.
headline: House Republicans try to open a $95 billion Iran-war lane
why_it_matters: War appropriations often clarify where slogans end and votes begin. If this advances, Republicans will have to defend both the spending and the conflict itself in an election-year environment.

body: Oil prices ticked higher after the United States reinstated its blockade on Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz, The New York Times reports. The key phrase is “tick higher”: the excerpt points to market movement, but not yet a panic. Still, shipping in the waterway has slowed to a crawl amid renewed warfare, putting energy traders back in the familiar position of pricing geopolitical risk through a chokepoint that can affect global supply expectations fast.
dek: A reinstated blockade on Iranian ports has slowed shipping in one of the world’s most sensitive waterways.
headline: Oil edges up as the U.S. renews pressure at Hormuz
why_it_matters: Hormuz is where regional conflict becomes everyone’s fuel bill. Even a modest oil move can widen if shipping disruptions persist or insurers, refiners and governments start behaving defensively.

body: The New York Times Magazine frames the American EV story starkly: the largest U.S. automakers have pulled back from electric vehicles while global sales are booming. The piece’s warning is existential rather than cyclical—this retreat may make them obsolete. From the excerpt alone, we don’t get the evidence trail or the counterargument from automakers, so the claim should be read as a forceful diagnosis, not a settled verdict. But the strategic tension is clear: domestic hesitation in a global growth market can harden into industrial disadvantage.
dek: U.S. automakers are backing away from electric vehicles just as global demand keeps growing.
headline: Detroit’s EV retreat looks less like caution than surrender
why_it_matters: If the future of car demand is increasingly electric, a retreat by U.S. incumbents is not just a product-planning miss. It risks ceding manufacturing scale, software learning and brand relevance to competitors abroad.

body: Interconnects calls the current moment “the most serious test to date” for open-source AI’s viability. The headline—“6 months to live for open models”—is intentionally dramatic, and from the supplied excerpt we should treat it as analysis, not a reported deadline. Still, the framing captures a real anxiety in the AI ecosystem: open models have to keep proving they can compete as frontier development becomes more expensive, more infrastructure-dependent and more strategically guarded.
dek: An Interconnects essay argues the next six months will test whether open-source AI can remain viable.
headline: Open AI models get their do-or-die forecast
why_it_matters: Open models are the counterweight to a closed AI stack controlled by a few labs and cloud companies. If they fall too far behind, experimentation, auditing and independent deployment all get narrower.

body: China’s economy grew 4.3 percent in the second quarter from a year earlier, its slowest pace in years, according to The New York Times. The important nuance is the split inside the number: export-oriented manufacturing remains a source of strength, while the broader economy is described as slumping. That suggests China’s familiar playbook—leaning on factories and foreign demand—may be masking softer conditions elsewhere rather than solving them.
dek: Second-quarter GDP rose 4.3 percent from a year earlier, with weakness broadening beyond manufacturing bright spots.
headline: China’s growth slows outside the export machine
why_it_matters: A slower China changes the global baseline for commodities, consumer demand and corporate earnings. If exports are carrying more of the load, Beijing’s growth problem also becomes more exposed to trade friction abroad.

body: The Times review of Ambassadors Clubhouse in New York is short in the supplied material but vivid: a “high-flying” outpost where the Punjabi cooking is “beguiling” and the city feels miles away. That is restaurant-critic shorthand for something more than competence. The appeal seems to be atmospheric as much as culinary—a room that can briefly override its own Manhattan context. The second source is essentially a duplicate listing of the same review, so there is no independent second take here.
dek: The New York outpost is reviewed as high-flying, beguiling and transportive.
headline: Ambassadors Clubhouse makes a case for escape-by-Punjabi-cooking
why_it_matters: In a city full of expensive dining rooms, transport is the trick everyone claims and few deliver. This review suggests Ambassadors Clubhouse may be one of the places actually pulling it off.

body: Archaeologists have unearthed ruins of a 2,400-year-old complex that suggest ancient Greeks treated Odysseus as more than a character in a poem, The New York Times reports. The proposed interpretation is striking: Homer’s protagonist may have been a figure of genuine devotion. The excerpt does not give the site details or the evidentiary debate, so “suggest” is doing real work here. But even cautiously framed, the find blurs the modern line between literature, myth and religion.
dek: A 2,400-year-old sanctuary points to Homer’s hero as an object of devotion in antiquity.
headline: Odysseus may have had worshippers, not just readers
why_it_matters: Ancient stories were not merely texts to be analyzed; they could structure places, rituals and identities. A sanctuary for Odysseus would make the Odyssey’s afterlife materially visible.
